Developer mode

Contents

  • Create a Github account
  • Fork the original github repository
  • Clone the github repository into your workspace
  • Get familiar with git commands withing the repository
  • Create a branch to work on
  • Making and pushing changes
  • Making a pull request

Developer mode#

If you plan to contribute to this library you can follow the follow steps pretty much inspired in https://docs.github.com/en/get-started/exploring-projects-on-github/contributing-to-a-project:

Create a Github account#

You can create an account in Github (https://github.com/signup). It is preferable having a student account using the university email.

Fork the original github repository#

Go to the public repository page on GitHub: https://github.com/oceanicos/oceanicospy and click the Fork button (top-right corner). This creates your own copy of the repo under your GitHub account.

Example: If the original repo is:

https://github.com/original-owner/project

After forking, your copy might be:

https://github.com/your-username/project

You should be selected as the owner of the forked repo

Clone the github repository into your workspace#

You’ve successfully forked the repository, but so far, it only exists on GitHub. To be able to work on the project, you will need to clone it to your computer (personal git or HPC system using). Type the following command into your terminal replacing your username. Please navigate to the folder where you want to install the repo:

git clone git@github.com:YOUR-USERNAME/oceanicospy.git

You might need to have your ssh keys already set up in your workspace and linked with your github account, so make sure you have it or create one: https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/connecting-to-github-with-ssh/adding-a-new-ssh-key-to-your-github-account

You can also use HTTPS protocol but use of SSH is encouraged. If you do not have the SSH keys linked with your github account you will not be able to push or pull commits into the repository workflow.

This creates a folder in your selected directory with the repository name.

Get familiar with git commands withing the repository#

Once you have the repository installed in your workspace you will be able to see a .git folder that indicates that this directory is a github repository. You are encouraged to check out different useful git commands. For instance:

git branch # Listing the current local branches

As you can see, Git by default only fetches the default branch, not all the branches.

git fetch origin

You can check the remote repository linked to your local one:

git remote -v

Create a branch to work on#

Before making changes to the project, you should create a new branch and check it out. By keeping changes in your own branch, you follow GitHub flow and ensure that it will be easier to contribute to the same project again in the future.

git branch BRANCH-NAME
git checkout BRANCH-NAME

A short, descriptive branch name enables your collaborators to see ongoing work at a glance. For example, increase-test-timeout or add-code-of-conduct. We’ll start naming the branch with our usernames before we deep into the git environment and learn properly the worflow.

Making and pushing changes#

Go ahead and make a few changes to the project using your favorite text editor. When you’re ready to submit your changes, stage and commit your changes. git add . tells Git that you want to include all of your changes in the next commit. git commit takes a snapshot of those changes.

git add .
git commit -m "a short description of the change"

When you stage and commit files, you essentially tell Git, “Take a snapshot of my changes”. You can continue to make more changes and take more commit snapshots.

Right now, your changes only exist locally. When you’re ready to push your changes up to GitHub, push your changes to the remote.

git push origin BRANCH-NAME

Making a pull request#

Creating a pull request is the final step in producing a fork of oceanicospy. When you’ve made a beneficial change and want to propose it to the original repository, you’ll create a pull request for a maintainer to review.

To do so, navigate to the repository on GitHub where your project lives. For this example, it would be at https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/oceanicospy. You’ll see a banner indicating that your branch is one commit ahead of oceanicos:main. Click Contribute and then Open a pull request.

GitHub will bring you to a page that shows the differences between your fork and the oceanicos/oceanicospy repository. Click Create pull request.

GitHub will bring you to a page where you can enter a title and a description of your changes. It’s important to provide as much useful information and a rationale for why you’re making this pull request in the first place. The project owner needs to be able to determine whether your change is as useful to everyone as you think it is. Finally, click Create pull request.

  • Fork the original github repository
  • Clone the github repository into your workspace
  • Get familiar with git commands withing the repository

  • Making and pushing changes
